text: This video presents a multivisceral resection carried out robotically for a sixty seven year old female who presented with a T four N one tumor.
The surgeon staples off the involved segment of the small bowel, leaving it attached to the specimen for removal.
The DaVinci X robotic system is utilized to divide the small bowel using a Shoreform X-T stapler with a blue cartridge. The small bowel mesentery is divided using a robotic vessel sealer.
The surgeon uses the stapler for a second firing to divide the efferent loop of the small bowel, completing the separation of this section.
The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, identifying the iliac vessels, ureter, and inferior mesenteric artery, with these anatomical structures clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection below the inferior mesenteric artery at the sacral promontory, proceeding cranially towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta.

The surgeon uses Endo-wrist instruments on a stable platform to enhance visualization of the sympathetic nerves, facilitating safe dissection around the region of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery, applying Hem-o-lok clips from the assistant port on the right side before dividing the artery.
The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric vein, defining the anatomy around the DJ flexure. The dissection plane is established directly beneath the inferior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection to separate the colonic mesentery from Girota's fascia, with the assistant providing counter-traction on the paranephric fat to aid the process.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) after placing hemlock clips, performing a high division of the IMV located beneath the pancreas.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection to separate the colon, advancing into the lesser sac located anterior to the pancreas.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection to separate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) from the gonadal vessels and left ureter. Visible inflammation and edema are present in the dissection plane due to the cancer and tumor desmoplasia.
The surgeon performs a lateral release of the left colon and sigmoid, using traction and counter-traction to facilitate the dissection from underneath, making the procedure visually straightforward.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing instrument to finalize the adhesion between the spleen and the colon, employing bipolar energy to securely divide vessels up to seven millimeters in size.

The surgeon shifts focus to the pelvis, performing posterior dissection of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The tumor visibly involves the bladder, uterus, and small bowel anteriorly, facilitating the dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection in the pre-sacral space, revealing the right inferior hypogastric nerve at the five o'clock position and the bladder visibly involved with the cancer.
The surgeon makes a full thickness entry into the bladder, revealing a malignant fistula connecting to the colon. The Foley's catheter is visible within the bladder lumen.

The surgeon uses a gas sealing device to divide the round ligament and ovarian particle on the right side. The uterus, which contains large fibroids, is prepared for an en bloc hysterectomy. The right corner is dissected, revealing the bladder and uterus.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side to mobilize the left half of the colon and divides the left tubo-ovarian pedicle using a vessel sealing instrument. The uterus, containing large fibroids, is prepared for en bloc removal.

The surgeon completes bladder dissection using a vessel sealing device. The uterus is pulled upwards to develop a plane behind the bladder and uterus, with minor bleeding occurring during these extra anatomical dissections. An assistant uses active suction to maintain a clear surgical field.

The specimen is mobilized cranially, creating more space in the pelvis for the mesorectal transection. The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to perform a single firing of a sixty shoreform stapler, transecting the rectum. Any remaining adhesions are divided, completely releasing the specimen.
The final view of the pelvis shows both ureters clearly visible.

The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a running 3-0 V-lock suture. The bladder is sutured in two layers with a 3-0 V-lock suture, ensuring the transection line near the trigone is secure. Both ureters are visibly stented to prevent accidental capture during suturing. Endo-wrist instruments facilitate the suturing process.
The operating time for this case was three hundred minutes. The blood loss was fifty mils.

The surgeon creates an end colostomy in the left iliac fossa, visible as a stoma on the abdominal surface, due to the advanced nature of the patient's tumor. The colostomy is intended for potential reversal if the patient remains tumor-free.

Histology of the resected bowel was consistent with a T-IV-BN-Zero tumor of her rectosigmoid junction with complete R-Zero resection. Twenty-six lymph nodes were harvested and were clear. Patient was admitted to surgical high care unit for twenty four hours and then transferred to the ward where she stayed for four days.
